coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for real-time colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensing ClO−/GSH
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huiqing
Wei, Zhenni
Zuo, Xianwei
Chen, Hongli
Ren, Cuiling
Dong, Yalei
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite (ClO−) and antioxidants have been reported closely correlated with oxidative stress and related diseases. In recent years, many probes, especially fluorescent and colorimetric probes, have been developed to detect ClO− and antioxidants. It is meaningful to develop probes which can be used to detect them in these two modes. Herein, a novel dual mode carbon dots (DM-CDs) which can rapidly respond to ClO− and antioxidants by both fluorescent and colorimetric mode were synthesized. GSH was chosen as model antioxidants to evaluate its detection performance. The fluorescence of DM-CDs can be quenched by ClO− and then recovered by GSH, so sensitive and accurate detection of ClO− and GSH can be achieved. Meanwhile, the colour variation of this system can be observed by naked eye and detected quantitatively by UV–Vis spectra. The excellent analytical performance of DM-CDs together with their good biocompatibility endow them with great feasibility in cell imaging and real-time detection of intracellular ClO− and GSH.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ClO− and GSH using DM-CDs
Detection of ClO− in fluorescent and colorimetric mode
Detection of GSH in fluorescent and colorimetric manner
Fluorescent for imaging ClO− and GSH in living cells
Detection of ClO− and GSH in biological samples
Possible mechanism of the optical response

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874061 , 21864003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of DM-CDs. (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution of DM-CDs); (B) FT-IR spectrum of DM-CDs; High resolution XPS spectra of (C) C 1s; (D) N 1s; (E) B 1s and (F) S 2p of the DM-CDs.

(A) FL emission spectra of DM-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of ClO− from 0 to 300 μM (Inset: relationship between F0/F and the concentration of ClO−). (B) UV–Vis spectra of the DM-CDs with the addition of 1–500 μM ClO−. (Inset: the linear plots of the absorbance and ClO− concentration). (C) Photographs of DM-CDs under 365 nm irradiation with the addition of different concentrations of ClO−, from left to right is 0, 1, 30, 60, 150, 250, 350 and 500 μM. (D) Photographs of DM-CD solution with the addition of different concentrations of ClO− (from left to right: 0, 1, 30, 60, 100, 150, 200, 250, 350, 400 and 500 μM) under sunlight.

(A) FL intensity (F/F0) of DM-CDs after the addition of various metal ions (Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, 3 mM), amino acids (Lys, Arg, Glu, Thr, 3 mM), active oxidizing species (H2O2, 3 mM; ONOO‾, ButOOH, 1.5 mM; 1O2, 1 mM, black bars), other chlorate anions (ClO2 −, ClO3 −, ClO4 −, 3 mM) (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 300 μM ClO‾ (red bars). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DM-CDs after the addition of ClO‾ and different interfering substances (C) Photographs of DM-CDs with ClO‾ and different interfering substances under sunlight.
(A) FL emission spectra of the mixture of DM-CDs and ClO−
(150 μM) upon the addition of various concentrations of GSH from 0 to 300 μM (Inset: relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of GSH). (B) UV–Vis absorbance of the mixture of DM-CDs and ClO−
(300 μM) with the addition of 1–200 μM GSH. (Inset: the linear plots of the absorbance corresponding to GSH concentration). (C) Photographs of DM-CDs and ClO−
(300 μM) with the addition of different concentrations of GSH (from left to right: 0, 3, 10, 30, 60, 150 and 300 μM) under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (D) Photographs of DM-CDs and ClO‾
(300 μM) solution with the addition of different concentrations of GSH (from left to right: 0, 3, 10, 20, 50, 80,150, and 300 μM) under sunlight.

Fluorescence microscopy images of RAW264.7 cells (A) and HeLa cells (B). From left to right: (a) RAW264.7 or HeLa cells pretreated with DM-CDs (0.3 mg mL−1) for 6 h; (b) then left for 6 h after pre-incubation with NaClO (200 μM) for 30 min; (c) finally incubated with 300 μM GSH for 30 min.

Fluorescence microscopy images of RAW264.7 cells (A) and HeLa cells (B). From left to right: (a) RAW264.7 or HeLa cells pretreated with DM-CDs (0.3 mg mL−1) for 6 h; (b) RAW264.7 or HeLa cells incubated with LPS (2 μg mL−1) for 8 h, PMA (2 μg mL−1) for 1 h and then incubated with DM-CDs (0.3 mg mL−1) for 6 h. (c) after that incubated with 300 μM GSH for 30 min.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of DM-CDs with 0, 70, 150, and 300 μM ClO−. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DM-CDs (black line), DM-CDs with ClO−(red line) and DM-CDs with ClO−and GSH (blue line). (C) FT-IR spectrum of DM-CDs (black line), DM-CDs + ClO−(red line) and DM-CDs + ClO−+GSH (blue line).
Synthesis of DM-CDs and its sensing performance for colorimetric/fluorescent detection of ClO− and GSH.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for real-time colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensing ClO−/GSH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: .State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institutes for Food and Drug Control/NMPA Key Laboratory for Researching and Evaluation of Cosmetics, Beijing, 100050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institutes for Food and Drug Control/NMPA Key Laboratory for Researching and Evaluation of Cosmetics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: .National Institutes for Food and Drug Control/NMPA Key Laboratory for Researching and Evaluation of Cosmetics, Beijing 100050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Sensor Technology, Gansu Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: .Institute of Sensor Technology, Gansu Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) and antioxidants have been reported closely correlated with oxidative stress and related diseases. In recent years, many probes, especially fluorescent and colorimetric probes, have been developed to detect ClO− and antioxidants. It is meaningful to develop probes which can be used to detect them in these two modes. Herein, a novel dual mode carbon dots (DM-CDs) which can rapidly respond to ClO− and antioxidants by both fluorescent and colorimetric mode were synthesized. GSH was chosen as model antioxidants to evaluate its detection performance. The fluorescence of DM-CDs can be quenched by ClO− and then recovered by GSH, so sensitive and accurate detection of ClO− and GSH can be achieved. Meanwhile, the colour variation of this system can be observed by naked eye and detected quantitatively by UV–Vis spectra. The excellent analytical performance of DM-CDs together with their good biocompatibility endow them with great feasibility in cell imaging and real-time detection of intracellular ClO− and GSH.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the indispensable reactive oxygen species (ROS) in biological system, [1] hypochlorite (ClO−) plays crucial roles in cellular redox balance and immune defence system. [2–4] However, abnormal level of ClO− can cause a range of diseases, including renal disease, cardiovascular diseases, reproductive failure, atherosclerosis, arthritis, lung injury and even tumor. [5–9] Meanwhile, the antioxidant defence system was existed to regulate the internal redox homeostasis. glutathione (GSH) is one of the most important antioxidants in living organisms, [10–14] it involves in different physiological processes by reacting with free radicals and enzymes. [15] Concentration variation of GSH could interrupt intracellular homeostasis and cause several diseases, like cancer, leukocyte loss, immune dysfunctions, cystic fibrosis as well as neurodegenerative illness. [16,17] So, it is necessary and critical to develop some sensors to investigate the variation of these redox substances rapidly.

To date, a number of methods for detecting hypochlorite and reducing substance have been developed, such as electrochemical assay, surface enhanced Raman scattering, chromatography, fluorescent method and colorimetric method. [18–28] Among the above methods, fluorescent method is considered to be a good candidate for its simple operation, high sensitivity, good penetration depth and easy to image in living cells. [29] Yoon et al. synthesized a two-photon fluorescent probe for ratiometric detection of ClO−. [30] Based on the synergistic operation mechanisms of aggregation induced emission (AIE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Zhang et al. reported a Zincke's salt-substituted tetraphenylethylene, which can detect GSH selectively and sensitively. [31] Meanwhile, colorimetric sensors were developed to realize rapid and convenient detection of ClO− and antioxidants. For example, Au nanospheres@Agnanorods was prepared by Wang et al. for colorimetric monitoring of ClO−. [28] Zheng et al. prepared a gold nanoparticles to detect glutathione and cysteine by visual chronometric. [32] ClO− and GSH can be analysized effectively by most of the reported fluorescent or colorimetric sensors, but nanosensors could realize fluorescent and colorimetric detection simultaneously are much-needed, because they can combine the advantages and unique features of the two methods. [24,33]

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained much research because of their high photostability, excellent hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, [34–38] which endowed them with extensive applications in chemical sensing, [39,40] bioimaging, [41] drug delivery, [42] and optoelectronic devices. [43,44]

On the basis of the above reason, it is meaningful to prepare fluorescent/colorimetric carbon dots for the detection of ClO− and GSH. So in this work, a new kind of dual mode carbon dots (DM-CDs) which can realize colorimetric and fluorescent detection of ClO− and GSH was prepared (Scheme 1 ). DM-CDs were obtained via a one-step hydrothermal approach, it emitted strong red fluorescence at 595 nm when excited at 520 nm, which can be quenched by ClO− and recovered by reducing substances, so it can be used as an “on-off-on” switchable sensor for sequential determination of ClO− and reductive substances.

2, 5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (DABS) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-Aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride (APAH) was bought from Adamas Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaClO·5H2O were obtained from TCI Development Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Tris (hydroxymthyl) aminomethane and acetic acid were acquired from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). NaCl, CaCl2, CoCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, and all amino acids were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium molybdate was acquired from Shanghai colloidal chemical factory (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments. Material purification was carried out using 1.0 kDa ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (Amicon Ultra-4, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the DM-CDs were measured using a FEI Talos instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, America) spectroscopy was conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were measured using a PerkinElmer PHI-5702 multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer. UV–visible absorption spectra were performed on a TU-1901 double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescent (FL) spectra were carried out using an RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a Xe lamp using 5/5 nm slit width (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Zeta potentials data were collected by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). Time-resolved FL spectra were obtained using a time-correlated single-photo counting (TCSPC) system from an FL 920 spectrometer. Living cell images and time-lapsing were acquired by fluorescence microscopy (ZEISS Z1) and Leica SP8X.

DM-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.1125 g of DABS and 0.0375 g APAH were dissolved in 15 mL of DI water, the solution was transferred into a teflon autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and filtered through ultrafiltration centrifuge tube. The as-synthesized DM-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− and GSH using DM-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: DM-CDs solution was prepared by 2.9 mL buffer solution (Tris-HAc, 10 mM, pH 7.4) and 100 μL DM-CDs (10 mg mL−1). The detection performance of DM-CDs towards ClO− was evaluated by measuring the FL and UV–Vis spectra of DM-CDs solution after adding different concentrations of ClO−. GSH responding experiment (both fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra) was carried out in the same conditions except the presence of ClO−. The interference and anti-interference assay of DM-CDs toward ClO− were carried out by adding different interfering substances in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively.

Serum samples and rat brain microdialysates provided by the General Hospital of Lanzhou Military Region were adopted to investigate the feasibility of DM-CDs for the analysis of ClO− and GSH in real biological samples. Authors state that all experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. The serum and rat brain microdialysates were diluted 100-fold before detection. The concentrations of ClO− and GSH in the diluted samples were analyzed by standard addition method. The FL emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength at 520 nm. All experiments were carried out under the same conditions.

The cytotoxicity of DM-CDs was examined through MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. Briefly, 100 μL of RAW 264.7 or HeLa cells were plated at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well in 96-well plastic plate and kept at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were exposed to various concentrations of DM-CDs for another 12 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well. After 4 h, the supernatant was discarded, followed by the addition of 100 μL DMSO into each well. Subsequently, the absorbance values were recorded by a microplate reader (Infinite M200, Switzerland) at 570 nm. The cell viability was calculated according to the following formula: Cell Viability (%) = mean Abs value of treatment group/mean Abs value of control.

RAW 264.7 or HeLa cells were placed on a 12-well plate and cultured in high-glucose DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h. After that, the cells were treated with DM-CDs for 6 h. Then, the cells in each well were rinsed with PBS (pH = 7.4). Finally, the cells were imaging using fluorescence microscope (ZEISS Z1). The uptake of ClO− were assessed, RAW 264.7 or HeLa cells were incubated with DM-CDs for 6 h as mentioned above, endogenous or exogenous ClO− was subsequently added to the cell-culture medium and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. For assessing GSH uptake, exogenous GSH was added after the addition of ClO−.

Time-lapse imaging of ClO− and GSH fluctuation was performed by a Leica SP8X and fluorescence images were obtained every 5 s. RAW264.7 or HeLa Cells were seeded on 15 mm glass dish including 2 mL of culture medium (DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS) at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h, followed by incubation with DM-CDs for 6 h. Finally, the cells were thoroughly rinsed with PBS for real time imaging: (1) 0–5 s, DM-CDs; (2) 6–15 s, DM-CDs + ClO−; (3) 15–29 s: DM-CDs + ClO−+GSH.

The optical properties of DM-CDs were influenced by reaction time, temperature and reagent dosage. Therefore, these conditions were optimized in order to obtain CDs with optimal properties. The emission intensity of DM-CDs reached maximum after reacted for 6 h (Fig. S1a). When the reaction conducted between 140 and 200 °C, the highest FL intensity was obtained at 180 °C (Fig. S1b). Thus, 180 °C and 6 h was chosen as the optimal reaction temperature and time, respectively. Based on the FL emission wavelength and intensity, the optimal reagent ratio of DABS and APAH were chosen as 3:1 (Figs. S1c and S1d).

The morphology and particle size of DM-CDs were determined using TEM. The particles are almost spherical (Fig. 1 A) with an average size about 4.3 nm according to the insets of Fig. 1A. Their detailed structure can be observed in high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (insets of Fig. 1A), the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was similar to that of graphite 100. [45,46]. FT-IR spectrum was used to investigate their structure and functional groups. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, the absorbance in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 was assigned to O–H and N–H bonds, [47] and the strong signal at 2591 cm−1 indicated the presence of –SH vibrations, demonstrating DM-CDs contain sulfur. [48] The sharp absorbance peak at 1603 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CC and CN stretching in the conjugated structure, [27,49] and the peak at 1495 cm−1 was belongs to the stretching vibrations of CC. The mode at 1582 cm−1 corresponded to N–H bending vibration. The band at 1404 cm−1 was related to C–N and C–S stretching vibration.[50] The peak at 1136 cm−1 was C–OH vibrations. [51] While B–O–H bending vibration, C–B stretching motions and B–O–H deformation vibration can be characterized by the absorption bands centered at 1194, 1092, and 1025 cm−1, respectively. [52] Valence state and elements composition of DM-CDs were explored by XPS, the full scan spectrum (Fig. S2) clearly proved the elemental composition of DM-CDs was C, O, N, S and B. High resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1C) suggested the presence of C–B (283.9 eV), CC/C–C (284.6 eV), C–N (285.5 eV), and C–O/C–S (285.9 eV). [53–55] Two dominant peaks at 398.9 and 399.6 eV in the XPS spectrum of N 1s were attributed to pyridinic N, and pyrrolic N (Fig. 1D), respectively. [51] The B 1s XPS spectrum of DM-CDs (Fig. 1E) can be well fitted into B–C (190.5 eV), B–O (195.2 eV), and B–S (196.6 eV). [56–58] As for the S2p spectrum of DM-CDs (Fig. 1F), the peaks at 167.4, 168.0, and 169.0 eV can be described to -C-SOx (x = 2, 3, and 4) species. [59,60] These results confirmed that the DM-CDs possess abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surfaces, which is in consistent with the FT-IR spectra.

The remarkable optical properties of DM-CDs were analyzed by UV–Vis and FL spectra. The absorption band of DM-CDs at 236 and 296 nm were ascribed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2domains and n-π* transitions of CO bonds (Fig. S3a). [61] DM-CDs exhibit another adsorption peaks at around 500 nm, which may be correlated with the red FL emission. [62] The emission peak shows no obvious change with the excitation wavelength increased from 380 to 520 nm (Fig. S3b), and the strongest emission peak appeared at 595 nm with excitation at 520 nm, this excitation-independent fluorescence behavior illustrates the rather uniform surface states of DM-CDs. [63,64]

The stability of DM-CDs towards light irradiation, ionic strength, temperature and pH were also investigated. The FL intensity remained unchanged under light illumination for 1 h (Fig. S4a). Fig. S4b demonstrates that even the concentration of NaCl was up to 100 mM, the fluorescent intensity of DM-CDs remained relatively stable. As presented in Fig. S4c, the FL intensity kept constant basically when the temperature increased from 20 to 50 °C. The above results indicating DM-CDs had excellent stability. Furthermore, Fig. S4d illustrates the FL intensity of DM-CDs were relative stable in the pH range of 4.0–8.0.

In this work, DABS (2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid) and APAH (2-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride) were used to prepare DM-CDs. In order to explore the effect of the structures and functional groups of the reagents on the photo-properties of DM-CDs, a series of control experiments were conducted (Fig. S5). If APAH was substituted by phenylboronic acid or aniline under the same conditions, the FL intensity of DM-CDs (Fig. S5, line a) was stronger than that of the CDs prepared by DABS and phenylboronic acid or aniline (Fig. S5, line b and c), confirmed the amino and boric acid groups of APAH played a significant role in improving the FL performance of DM-CDs. Meanwhile, effects of sulfonic and amino groups provided by DABS on adjusting the optical property of DM-CDs were also studied. When p-phenylenediamine or 2-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was used to prepare CDs, the products exhibited very weak emission intensity (Fig. S5, line d and e), suggests the sulfonic and amino of DABS also made great contribution to the high fluorescent intensity of DM-CDs. Moreover, the carbon dots prepared by DABS and aniline showed the similar color response to ClO− and GSH as DM-CDs. From the above results, we speculate that the combined effect of the amino group of APAH and the sulfonic and amino groups provided by DABS endow the colorimetric detection ability of DM-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− in fluorescent and colorimetric mode
ce:section.ce:para: The significant advantages of DM-CDs make it a good candidate for the detection of ClO−. In the fluorescent mode, the emission intensity at 595 nm was decreased as the amount of ClO− increased (Fig. 2 A). A significant linear correlation (F0/F = 0.0106 [ClO‾]+1.006) was displayed between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentration of ClO− in the range of 0.3–300 μM (R2 = 0.994), (where the FL intensity of DM-CDs in the absence and presence of ClO− were represented by F0 and F, respectively) and the limit of detection was calculated to be as low as 89 nM based on 3δ/K rule (δ is the standard deviation of 10 blank signals and K represents the slope of linear equation) (inset in Fig. 2A). Furthermore, the colorimetric sensing of ClO− can also be realized by DM-CDs. The absorbance at 662 nm was increased gradually with increasing the concentration of ClO− (Fig. 2B), and an excellent linear relationship (A/A0 = 0.4940 [ClO−]-0.7288) between A/A0 and the concentration of ClO−was obtained in the range of 1–500 μM (R2 = 0.999) with a detection limit of 0.45 μM (inset in Fig. 2B). The corresponding pictures of DM-CDs solution with different concentrations of ClO− under UV lamp and sunlight were shown in Fig. 2C and D. It can be seen that the fluorescence of DM-CDs was decreased gradually upon the addition of ClO−, meanwhile, the solution color changed from pink to dark blue distinctively, which can be observed clearly by naked eye.

Since the real biological system is rather complex, the interference and anti-interference assay of DM-CDs for the detection of ClO− were investigated by adding other interfering substances, including others ROS (1O2, ONOO−, H2O2, ButOOH), metal cations (Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+), amino acids (Lys, Arg, Glu, Thr), and other chlorate anions (ClO2 −, ClO3 −, ClO4 −) (Fig. 3 A). In addition, UV–Vis spectra of DM-CDs in the presence of ClO− appeared a new absorption band at 662 nm in contrast to other substances (Fig. 3B). Moreover, Fig. 3C illustrate these substances do not trigger color change. The above mentioned results demonstrated that ClO− can be detected selectively by DM-CDs through colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in fluorescent and colorimetric manner
ce:section.ce:para: The FL intensity of DM-CDs can be quenched by ClO− and restored by some specific reductants, such as GSH, ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys) and Na2S2O4 (Fig. S6). Among them, GSH was selected as model material to assess the sensing performance of DM-CDs. The fluorescence of the mixture of DM-CDs and ClO− (150 μM) can be recovered by GSH. As shown in Fig. 4 A, The emission intensity at 595 nm was increased after the addition of GSH and a good linear relationships (F0/F = −0.0116 [GSH]+0.9545, F0/F = −0.0005 [GSH]+0.7335) between F0/F and GSH concentration in the range of 0.3–20 μM (R2 = 0.991) and 20–300 μM (R2 = 0.995) was obtained, with a detection limit of 120 nM (inset in Fig. 4A). In the meantime, the colorimetric sensing performance was also studied. The absorbance at 662 nm was decreased after the addition of GSH (Fig. 4B), good linear correlation (A/A0 = −0.0105 [GSH]+1.0130) between the absorbance and the concentration of GSH could be obtained in the range of 3–80 μM (R2 = 0.994), and the detection limit was calculated to be 1.81 μM (inset in Fig. 4B). The photograph of the mixture of DM-CDs and ClO‾ with different concentrations of GSH solution under UV light and sunlight were shown in Fig. 4C and D. The fluorescence signal of the mixture could be gradually recovered by increasing the concentrations of GSH, and the color of mixed solution of DM-CDs and ClO− changed from dark blue to pink. The fluorescent and colorimetric detection performance for ClO− and GSH by DM-CDs was comparable with the previously reported sensors (Table S1, Table S2). The FL intensity and color of DM-CDs changed by ClO− and recovered by GSH were shown in Videos S1 and S2 (accelerated version). Furthermore, the time duration of the detection process is relatively short (Fig. S7), which endows this sensor can be used to real time detect ClO− and GSH both by fluorescent and colorimetric manners.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110614.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent for imaging ClO− and GSH in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110614.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3
Multimedia component 4 Multimedia component 4

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− and GSH in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, DM-CDs were applied for detecting ClO− and GSH in serum and rat brain microdialysates by standard addition method. As listed in Table S3, Table S4, satisfactory recoveries and accuracy for the determination of ClO− and GSH can be obtained. Moreover, the fluorescence and colorimetric sensing photos of ClO− and GSH in serum and rat brain microdialysates by DM-CDs were shown in Figs. S10 and S11. These results further confirmed the reliability and feasibility of using DM-CDs for dual-mode sensing of ClO− and GSH in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the optical response
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanism was also studied in this work. A typical Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1+Ksv[ClO−]) was obtained. Meanwhile, Fig. 7 A illustrates the fluorescence decay time of DM-CDs was almost unchanged when the concentration of ClO− changed from 0 to 300 μmol L−1. These results suggested the fluorescence of DM-CDs was quenched by ClO− through static quenching. UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 7B) illustrate DM-CDs showed a new absorption band centered at 662 nm in the presence of ClO−, which was disappeared after the addition of GSH. Given that, oxidation and reduction of the surface functional groups of DM-CDs by ClO− and GSH may be responsible for the fluorescent and colorimetric responding. Fig. 7C illustrates the response current increased to reach a steady state with the addition of ClO− and reduced steeply by GSH under constant potential. This result confirmed that the optical response of DM-CDs toward ClO− and GSH was correlated with their redox property. Meanwhile, FT-IR spectra of DM-CDs, DM-CDs + ClO− and DM-CDs + ClO−+GSH were compared in Fig. 7D, which illustrate the peaks of B–O–H, CC and C–B bond were disappearing after the addition of ClO−. Meanwhile, the C–OH on the surface of DM-CDs has a slight displacement. The above result illustrates the B–O–H, C–OH, CC and C–B bond of DM-CDs readily interacts with ClO−, which result in the fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, the disappeared peaks by ClO− was appeared after the addition of GSH, except CC bond, so the FL signal was recovered. According to the above results, the responding mechanism of DM-CDs towards ClO− and GSH was based on redox reaction.

In this work, a dual mode CDs (DM-CDs) was successfully prepared through one-pot hydrothermal method and it had excellent photostability, good water solubility and biocompatibility. Further-more, this sensor can be used to effectively and efficiently detect ClO− and GSH by colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode sequentially. More significantly, it can also be used as a optical probe for the detection of ClO− and GSH in living cells. This powerful fluorescence and colorimetric probe hold great potential in studying the treatment of several diseases that associated with ClO− and GSH.

Huiqing Li: contributed to the conception of the study, performed the experiment. Zhenni Wei: performed the experiment. Xianwei Zuo: contributed significantly to data analysis and manuscript preparation. Hongli Chen: helped perform the analysis with constructive discussions. Cuiling Ren: contributed to the conception of the study. Yalei Dong: contributed to the conception of the study, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, and wrote the manuscript. Xingguo Chen: helped perform the analysis with constructive discussions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874061, 21864003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 5 Multimedia component 5

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110614.
